Alerts & notifications
Critical — act now
Downtown Funding and Records Search Search BID assessments, DDA grants, TIF reimbursements, board meetings, and financial records in one place.
Annual adopted budget — C.R.S. 29-1-101 et seq · April 10, 2026
Open the search portal →
ADA Title II deadline: April 24, 2026 — 16 days. All local government websites must meet WCAG 2.1 AA. Zero district sites are confirmed compliant on PDF documents.
HB21-1110 · 28 C.F.R. Part 35 · April 8, 2026
Open district audit dashboard →
249 of 284 metro districts have no website. CORA request is the only way a resident can access their own district's records.
EPC Assessor audit · April 2026
See district audit →
WS Districts (WSDM) serves 30+ districts on a single WordPress site. Last calendar entry: 2021. No governance docs. No financials. One compliance letter fixes all 30+ simultaneously.
Platform audit · April 2026
Draft compliance letter →
High priority
All posted PDFs likely fail WCAG 1.3.1. Meeting minutes, budgets, and audit docs are almost certainly untagged. This is the real HB21-1110 failure — not the homepage.
ADA document audit · April 2026
View ADA analysis →
0 of 8 audited sites post their service plan. C.R.S. §32-1-202 requires it. This is the foundational legal document. Not one district links it publicly.
Governance gap · §32-1-202
See audit dashboard →
Annual audit documents: publicly posted by 0 of 8 districts. Required by §32-1-106. Due to State Auditor by July 31. The clock is running.
Governance gap · §32-1-106 · due July 31
See audit dashboard →
Informational
2 CORA requests pending response. Military housing numbers and district governance records both awaiting reply. Response window is still open.
CORA tracker · April 2026
View tracker →
Wolfersberger compliance and Bellamese analysis complete. New findings ready for review on developer agreements, compliance history, and parcel-level records.
Compliance analysis · April 2026
Read analysis →
Jurisdiction finder is live. Enter any El Paso County address to find your metro districts, school district, city jurisdiction, and utilities — plain language, no login.
New · April 2026
Find my jurisdiction →
CSPD Blotter — Weekly History is live. 271 incidents pulled directly from the live blotter feed, paired with a Poisson 95% confidence band to flag statistical anomalies week to week. Tabs are full APG-pattern keyboard accessible; every chart has an aria-label.
New · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open blotter dashboard →
Lorson Ranch financial dashboard is live. Seven cross-pledged metro districts, $94.2M aggregate audited deficit, IGA money flows, and a per-home tax calculator — sourced from 2026 budgets and 2024 audits. Companion plain-language briefing available as a download.
New · Special Districts · April 2026
Open Lorson Ranch dashboard →
CBI Missing Persons form — accessible rebuild is live. Resolves all seven critical/serious findings from the ADA audit: skip link, live regions, focus management, EN/ES localization, real-time validation messaging. Drops into the existing Drupal Webform infrastructure with optional Claude API routing.
New · State · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open CBI form rebuild →
LexisNexis ADAM Program dashboard — accessible rebuild is live. Audit and reference rebuild of the missing-children dashboard: APG-pattern tabs with roving tabindex and full keyboard nav, solid amber focus rings (3:1 against any surface), darker success-green for AA contrast, and "View Poster" placeholders converted from dead anchors to real buttons.
New · Private Sector · April 2026 · WCAG 2.1 AA
Open ADAM dashboard →

In immediate danger? This form is not for emergencies. Call 911 now, or contact your local FBI field office.

DOJ
U.S. Department of Justice Civil Rights Division
Secure transmission
File a civil rights complaint

Tell us what happened. We'll help you organize it.

Describe the civil rights violation in your own words — in one place, in plain language. We'll extract the details the Department of Justice needs and show them to you before you submit. No seven-step wizard. No forced categorization. Works on any device.

i

Some issues are faster to resolve elsewhere. Workplace discrimination goes to the EEOC, housing to HUD, air travel to DOT. Links are at the bottom of this page. If you're not sure, start here.

Step 1 of 2

What happened?

Write in your own words. Include who, what, when, and where if you know them. You can mention a disability, the date you encountered a barrier, the government office or website involved — we'll pick those out automatically and let you review before anything is submitted.

No character limit. Take your time.
0 characters
We extract locally in your browser. Nothing is sent yet.
Step 2 of 2 — Review and edit

Here's what we extracted from your description.

Every field is editable. The color-coded badges show what was extracted automatically (confident), partially extracted (review), or left for you to fill (manual). These fields mirror the seven steps on the civilrights.justice.gov form.

Plain-language summary
—
✓

Complaint submitted

The Department of Justice's Civil Rights Division has received your complaint. Review can take up to three months. You can call the ADA Information Line at 800-514-0301 (voice) or 1-833-610-1264 (TTY) for status.

Reference: DOJ-CRT-00000000

Is your issue one of these? There's a faster path.

The DOJ Civil Rights Division handles most civil rights violations, but some classes of complaint are better filed with a specialized agency. These agencies have dedicated staff and shorter review times for their subject area.

Employment
EEOC
Discrimination at work or when applying for a job — disability, race, religion, sex, age, national origin.
eeoc.gov →
Housing
HUD
Denied housing, accessible-unit refusals, reasonable-accommodation denials, or disability-based eviction.
hud.gov →
Air travel
Dept. of Transportation
Discrimination by a specific airline — wheelchair handling, service-animal denial, accessibility refusal.
transportation.gov →
Colorado residents
Colorado Civil Rights Division
State-law parallel route for Colorado complaints under CADA (C.R.S. § 24-34-301). Sometimes faster than federal.
ccrd.colorado.gov →
Advocacy support
Disability Law Colorado
Colorado's Protection & Advocacy organization. Legal representation, systemic advocacy, policy work on disability issues.
dlc.org →
Phone
ADA Information Line
Speak with a specialist. 800-514-0301 (voice), 1-833-610-1264 (TTY). Monday–Friday. Spanish available.
800-514-0301 →

The current form at civilrights.justice.gov/report is a seven-step wizard that displays a visible notice reading: "Your mobile browser is not compatible with this form." The form asks users to self-categorize their complaint before describing what happened, requires them to traverse multiple screens with separate field validations, and — as of April 2026 — fails several WCAG 2.1 Level AA success criteria that the Department's own rulemaking (28 C.F.R. Part 35 Subpart H, effective April 24, 2026) identifies as the benchmark. This redesign re-orders the interaction: describe first, extract second, review before submitting.

Natural-language primary input UX
One textarea replaces the seven-step wizard. Users with cognitive, attention, or memory disabilities do not have to hold field requirements in mind while also recalling the event. Field extraction happens after a complete description is written, not during it.
Mobile-first layout with no browser gatekeeping WCAG 4.1.1
Works on any modern browser including mobile. No "this form is not compatible with your browser" notice. No JavaScript-required hard block. The design tests cleanly on iOS Safari and Android Chrome with assistive-technology pairings.
Every form input has a programmatic label WCAG 1.3.1, 3.3.2, 4.1.2
No inputs rely on placeholder text or visual proximity alone. Screen-reader users hear each field's name and purpose. Required fields use aria-required; errors use role="alert".
Transparent extraction with source quotes Trust
When a field is auto-filled, the source quote from the user's description is shown beneath it. Users can verify exactly why a field was populated. No black-box inference; no AI-sounding confidence they can't interrogate.
Alternate-path cards surfaced before submit Routing
EEOC (employment), HUD (housing), DOT (air travel), Colorado CCRD, Disability Law Colorado, and the ADA Information Line are all linked directly. A user whose issue is workplace discrimination can reach EEOC without completing a DOJ complaint first.
Client-side extraction, no pre-submission tracking Privacy
Field extraction runs in the user's browser. Nothing is transmitted until the user clicks submit. The current civilrights.justice.gov form uses government-hosted infrastructure but relies on cloud storage for its page assets; this prototype demonstrates that extraction can be done locally with zero data leakage before consent.
Libre Baskerville + IBM Plex Mono + Inter Typography
A government civil-rights form should communicate institutional weight. Libre Baskerville (serif) for headlines carries that weight. IBM Plex Mono signals secure, official, structured data. Inter is a highly legible body face. The navy + gold palette derives from DOJ's own visual identity.
Anonymous submission is a primary option, not a buried fallback Accessibility / Trust
Anonymous filing is offered as a first-class checkbox on stage one, with clear explanation of what the user gives up (no status updates, no follow-up). Users reporting powerful institutions — or fearing retaliation — should not have to dig for the anonymous option.
